does: This paper identifies and addresses a credit assignment problem in reinforcement learning-based multi-turn jailbreak attacks against large language models. The authors observe that existing methods apply uniform trajectory-level rewards across all dialogue turns, but turns actually contribute non-uniformly to attack success depending on their phase and target. The paper proposes TRACE, a turn-aware credit assignment framework that differentiates turn-level contributions in successful trajectories through leave-one-turn-out semantic masking and assigns graduated penalties in failed trajectories based on prompt harmfulness, semantic relevance, and refusal detection. This granular approach improves both the efficiency of learning attack strategies and the quality of learned multi-turn jailbreak sequences.
connects: This work extends recent training-based approaches to adversarial attacks on LLMs, building on prior research that uses reinforcement learning to automatically generate jailbreak prompts. It responds to limitations in trajectory-level reward assignment seen in earlier RL-based attack methods and connects to the broader literature on multi-turn dialogue safety and adversarial robustness of conversational AI. The paper also engages with foundational work on credit assignment in reinforcement learning, applying established techniques like advantage attribution to the novel domain of multi-turn LLM attacks. The semantic masking approach relates to interpretability and importance estimation methods in deep learning.
enables: This work enables more efficient discovery of sophisticated multi-turn attack strategies by improving the learning signal for RL-based jailbreak optimization. The turn-aware framework could facilitate better understanding of which dialogue patterns and semantic shifts most effectively manipulate LLMs across extended conversations. The methodology may also inform defensive training approaches, since identifying which turns are most critical to attacks suggests where additional safety mechanisms should focus. Beyond attacks, the credit assignment insights could improve multi-turn dialogue systems more broadly by clarifying how contribution should flow across extended interactions, supporting development of safer and more interpretable conversational AI systems.
